items.myth: If read_csv succeeds without errors, the data loaded correctly.
items.reality: Silent issues are common: wrong dtypes, truncated leading zeros, or dates parsed as objects. Always inspect dtypes, null counts, and row counts against source metadata.
items.example: A 10M-row file loads with 9.8M rows because embedded newlines broke quoting—`len(df)` vs expected count catches this.

items.myth: Parquet and CSV are interchangeable—just change the extension.
items.reality: Parquet carries binary types and compression; CSV requires parsing text and explicit dtype arguments. Conversion paths differ and CSV round-trips can widen types.
items.example: Saving int32 Parquet to CSV and reloading without dtype may yield float64 columns if any missing values appear.

items.myth: You should always load only the training split from disk to prevent leakage.
items.reality: Loading the full dataset is fine when you immediately partition and ensure every fit step uses train only. The leak comes from statistics computed on all rows, not from the file read itself.
items.example: Load full `sales.parquet`, sort by date, take first 70% as train—valid. Fit StandardScaler on all rows before split—invalid.
